title: Endometriosis - NHS
text:

Surgery for endometriosis If other treatments are not working or you have lots of endometriosis, you may be offered surgery. Surgery is also an option if endometriosis is affecting your ability to have children.
You can have surgery to:
remove areas of endometriosis or fluid-filled sacs (ovarian cysts) caused by endometriosis remove your womb ( hysterectomy ) or ovaries (oophorectomy) remove part of your bladder or bowel, if endometriosis is affecting these You may need to have more surgeries if your symptoms come back after your first surgery, or if surgery causes scarring that makes your organs stick together (adhesions).
Support for living with endometriosis Treatment and support are also available for other problems caused by endometriosis.
Support you might need includes:
advice on ways to manage long-term pain and extreme tiredness (fatigue) referral to a fertility specialist if you have difficulty getting pregnant mental health support if you have low mood or anxiety 
Information: 
Find out more Endometriosis UK: treatments for endometriosis. Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists: endometriosis 
How endometriosis affects your life 
Endometriosis is different for everyone.
Treatment usually helps manage symptoms of endometriosis.
